items.q: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. What is the relationship between co-interior (allied) angles?
items.misconception: They are equal to each other, like alternate angles.
items.random_wrong: They add up to 360 degrees because they make a full turn.
items.why: Students lump all parallel-line angle relationships into 'equal' (because corresponding and alternate are equal), missing that co-interior angles sum to 180.

items.q: Can Pythagoras' theorem be used on any triangle?
items.correct: No — it only applies to right-angled triangles.
items.misconception: Yes — it works on any triangle as long as you square the sides.
items.random_wrong: Only on equilateral triangles because all sides are equal.
items.why: Students learn the formula a^2 + b^2 = c^2 abstractly and forget the essential prerequisite — a 90 degree angle.

items.misconception: 15, because 15% means 15 units out of any amount.
items.why: Students treat 'X percent of' as if X is the answer directly, not as a proportion to apply to the amount.

items.q: Angles in a triangle add to?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 180°. “360°” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Each angle in an equilateral triangle is?
items.why: Correct answer: 60°. “90°” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Triangle has angles 50° and 70°. What is the third? (degrees)
items.why: Exact answer: 60. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
